completeness.rationale: All seven expected extracts are present: delay duration (2-4 hours), onset (~1 week ago), morning-worse/afternoon-better pattern, connection to marketing digest batch process, queue congestion hypothesis, transactional vs. bulk emails sharing a pipeline, and pre-regression baseline (1-2 minutes). The summary also adds value with reproduction steps, a proposed test case, and explicit information gaps.

accuracy.rationale: The triage correctly identifies the daily digest feature as the cause and queue saturation as the mechanism, which matches acceptable path #2 exactly. While it doesn't name the specific priority inversion (digest emails at the same priority as transactional), it correctly diagnoses shared queue contention and recommends separating real-time notifications onto a higher-priority queue — which is precisely the fix. No factual errors are present.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked two focused rounds of questions and had solid evidence before resolving. It correctly flagged remaining unknowns (queue logs, digest volume, whether other self-hosted users are affected) in the information_gaps section. One obvious question was left unasked: how many users receive the digest (i.e., queue volume), which would have strengthened the hypothesis further. Resolving at this point was reasonable but slightly premature.

economy.rationale: Every question asked was essential. The first turn established environment and change history; the second turn targeted the specific timing correlation that confirmed the hypothesis. No redundant or low-value questions were asked.

question_quality.rationale: The second-round questions were genuinely diagnostic and elicited the critical insight — the reporter explicitly said they hadn't connected the digest timing to the delays until asked. Asking 'roughly what time of day do digest emails arrive?' and 'when a delayed notification finally comes through, does it arrive around the same time as the digest?' demonstrated real diagnostic reasoning and directly unlocked the root cause.

actionability.rationale: The summary is immediately actionable: it includes a specific root cause hypothesis, step-by-step reproduction instructions, a prioritized list of investigation steps (check shared queue, inspect queue depth at 9am, separate queues), a proposed test case with an SLA assertion, and explicit information gaps. A developer could begin coding a fix or writing a diagnostic script immediately.

notable_strengths: The second-round follow-up on digest timing and correlation was precisely targeted and elicited the key insight the reporter had not volunteered
The triage summary is exceptionally actionable — reproduction steps, specific fix recommendations, a proposed test case, and explicit information gaps all in one structured artifact
The agent correctly identified the digest feature as the cause in just two turns without any server-side data
notable_weaknesses: Did not ask how many users receive the daily digest, which would have quantified queue volume and further strengthened the congestion hypothesis
Did not ask whether disabling the digest temporarily resolves the delays — a quick workaround confirmation that would also serve as diagnostic validation
most_insightful_question: Roughly what time of day do the daily digest emails arrive? When a delayed notification finally comes through, does it arrive around the same time as the digest, or at a completely different time?
missed_opportunities: How many users are on the TaskFlow instance and receive the daily digest? (Would have revealed the ~10K email volume behind the congestion)
Have you tried disabling the daily digest to see if notification delays resolve? (Would confirm causation and provide an immediate workaround)
